explanation:
The natural language specifies that the system must implement the following operating modes: Start-up Mode, Self-test Mode, Standby Mode, Alarm Test Mode, PCV, PSV, and Fail-safe Mode. All of these correspond to atomic propositions except 'Alarm Test Mode', for which there is no matching atomic proposition in the provided list. The rest of the modes are present: StartUpMode, SelfTestMode, StandbyMode, PCVMode, PSVMode, failSafeMode.

The appropriate formalization is that at all times, the system is in at least one of these known valid modes, representing that the system is always in (or at least capable of) one of the required modes. We use template 7: Globally(bool_exp3), with bool_exp3 as the disjunction of all available mode variables. This best fits the intent of ensuring the implementation and continuing availability of the operating modes that have corresponding atomic propositions.
bool_exp3: StartUpMode | SelfTestMode | StandbyMode | PCVMode | PSVMode | failSafeMode

explanation: The original translation referenced 'AlarmTestMode', which is not a valid atomic proposition in the provided list. The correct approach is to represent the requirement that the system shall implement the enumerated operating modes, using only valid atomic propositions. From the list, the valid propositions identifying modes are: StartUpMode, SelfTestMode, StandbyMode, PCVMode, PSVMode, and failSafeMode. To formalize 'system shall implement these modes', we state that globally, at least one of these mode atomic propositions must be true at any time, ensuring the system is always in a valid required mode.
bool_exp3: StartUpMode | SelfTestMode | StandbyMode | PCVMode | PSVMode | failSafeMode

explanation: The natural language statement lists required operating modes for the system: Start-up, Self-test, Standby, Alarm Test, PCV, PSV, and Fail-safe. Each of these has a corresponding atomic proposition with a detailed description. The most fundamental way to capture their availability in LTL is to require that globally (i.e., at all times in the execution), there exists a moment when each mode is possible/entered/active. The simplest encoding is to state that, globally, the ability to enter at least one of these modes (combined with OR) is always possible, ensuring these modes are part of the system state space. While more nuanced, mode-transition logic would demand further detail about mutual exclusivity or transitions; this statement is a basic coverage requirement. Thus, we use the template: 'bool_exp3' (index 0), with bool_exp3 being the disjunction of all mode atomic propositions.
bool_exp3: StartUpMode | SelfTestMode | StandbyMode | PCVMode | PSVMode | failSafeMode

explanation: The natural language requires implementation and reachability of all specified operating modes: Start-up Mode, Self-test Mode, Standby Mode, Pressure Controlled Ventilation Mode (PCV), Pressure Support Ventilation Mode (PSV), and Fail-safe mode. Each of these modes is represented by a valid atomic proposition: StartUpMode, SelfTestMode, StandbyMode, PCVMode, PSVMode, and failSafeMode. To fulfill the requirement that all these modes are implemented and can be entered, an LTL property expressing that, eventually, at least one of these modes is reached is: F(StartUpMode | SelfTestMode | StandbyMode | PCVMode | PSVMode | failSafeMode). A more exhaustive form would use a conjunction of eventually (F) for each mode, but given template constraints, a disjunction best matches the notion of reachability encoded via one template. This specification provides evidence that all listed modes are accessible (i.e., at least one will be entered at some point), consistent with the requirement that the modes exist and are implemented.

The input describes seven specific operating modes that the system must implement. For LTL, this is best captured by specifying that each mode's proposition (e.g., StartUpMode, SelfTestMode, StandbyMode, AlarmTestMode, PCVMode, PSVMode, failSafeMode) is eventually (at some point) possible to activate, which implies implementability in the system. The LTL property 'F StartUpMode', 'F SelfTestMode', etc., can each be formulated. To capture that at least one mode must be eventually possible, we take their disjunction and require that eventually at least one of them is activated. I use the template for eventuality 'F bool_exp3', where bool_exp3 is a disjunction of all mode propositions. N_DURATION is not strictly required for this general eventuality, but for specification, I set it to 10 to meet template requirements, although this would normally be left unbounded. Thus, we capture: Eventually, at least one of the required operating modes is (or can be) active, corresponding to the system being able to enter each mode.

The requirement lists the operating modes (Start-up, Self-test, Standby, Alarm Test, PCV, PSV, Fail-safe) that shall be implemented in the system. To capture this at the LTL level, we can state globally that at any point in time, one of these mode atomic propositions should hold (i.e., the system is in one of the specified modes). Since not every atomic proposition directly matches the modes, but atomic propositions like StartUpMode, SelfTestMode, StandbyMode, PCVMode, PSVMode, and failSafeMode are present, we can use their disjunction. There is no explicit atomic proposition for 'Alarm Test Mode', but the requirement is best captured by ensuring that always (globally) the system is in at least one valid mode among those defined as atomic propositions. This meets the intent: these modes are available in the system at all times.
bool_exp3: StartUpMode | SelfTestMode | StandbyMode | PCVMode | PSVMode | failSafeMode

explanation: The requirement describes the existence of various operating modes in the system: Start-up, Self-test, Standby, Alarm Test, PCV, PSV, and Fail-safe Mode. Each of these modes has an associated atomic proposition in the dictionary (e.g., 'StartUpMode', 'SelfTestMode', 'StandbyMode', 'PCVMode', 'PSVMode', 'failSafeMode'). Since the requirement is simply to have these modes implementable and not to express timing or dependencies, the most direct translation is that at all times ('Globally'), at least one of these mode flags can be active, i.e., the system is always in at least one operating mode. This is captured with the LTL formula G (StartUpMode | SelfTestMode | StandbyMode | PCVMode | PSVMode | failSafeMode). For an enumerative requirement about the existence/implementation of distinct system modes, this is the most literal and appropriate LTL expression.

The input describes that the system shall implement several named operating modes. In the atomic proposition list, there are atomic propositions indicating the activation of specific modes, such as StartUpMode (Start-up Mode), SelfTestMode (Self-test Mode), StandbyMode (Standby Mode), PCVMode (Pressure Controlled Ventilation Mode), PSVMode (Pressure Support Ventilation Mode), and failSafeMode (Fail-safe mode). Since these are all required to exist as operational modes, the most fundamental property we can ensure in LTL is that it is always possible to reach (eventually) any of these essential modes at some future point during system operation. We capture this by requiring that, at some point, each mode becomes true (is entered). Since LTL formulas work best with finite expressions and always-eventuality can be ensured by combining a set of eventually (F) operators, but since the template set only allows for a single property, we list as the key property that it is always possible (at some point) to reach a state where any of these seven modes becomes active. Therefore, the best matching template is the "Eventually" (F) operator over the disjunction of each mode proposition, ensuring at least one can be reached. Assign N_DURATION=10 for a bounded future eventuality.
bool_exp3: StartUpMode | SelfTestMode | StandbyMode | PCVMode | PSVMode | failSafeMode

explanation: The natural language requirement describes that the system shall implement multiple operating modes: Start-up, Self-test, Standby, Alarm Test, PCV, PSV, and Fail-safe. In the atomic propositions, there are specific atomic propositions for the main modes (StartUpMode, SelfTestMode, StandbyMode, PCVMode, PSVMode, failSafeMode), each indicating the system is in that mode. A straightforward way to encode the requirement that these modes are indeed implemented (i.e., possible/available) is to assert that, eventually, the system is able to reach each of these modes. LTL expresses this as an 'eventually' (F) operator for each mode. However, with the available templates, the closest match is to state that, globally (at all times), it is possible eventually to reach one of these modes, i.e., 'F (StartUpMode | SelfTestMode | StandbyMode | PCVMode | PSVMode | failSafeMode)'. This property ensures that, from any state, the system can still reach any of the listed main operating modes, i.e., they exist as system states and are accessible from anywhere, which best captures the intent of 'the system shall implement' these modes.

The natural language requirement is that the system implements all seven specified operating modes. The best way to capture that each mode is reachable—that is, that each mode can eventually be entered by the system—is to require that, for each mode, it is eventually possible for the corresponding atomic proposition to become true at some point in the system's execution. Given the available templates and the fact that atomic propositions are not by themselves boolean constants, the only way to encode this over the given templates is to translate the requirement separately for each mode using 'F bool_exp3' with each of the atomic propositions, then combine the results externally (outside the provided template set) if needed. Here, we show the top (most faithful) template for the StartUpMode; the same logic applies for the other six, and they are listed each as a separate entry.
